# Agent Armor

> Open-source security framework for AI agents. Detects and defends against AI Agent Traps.

## What is Agent Armor?

Agent Armor is a TypeScript middleware library that scans text content for adversarial attacks targeting AI agents. It sits in your agent pipeline at three interception points: pre-ingestion (user input), post-retrieval (RAG chunks), and pre-execution (LLM output).

It detects threats based on the Google DeepMind Agent Trap taxonomy (Franklin et al., 2026).

## Install

```
npm install @stylusnexus/agentarmor
```

## What it detects

- Content Injection: hidden HTML instructions, metadata injection, dynamic cloaking, syntactic masking
- Behavioural Control: jailbreak patterns, data exfiltration attempts, unauthorized sub-agent spawning
- Cognitive State: RAG poisoning, memory poisoning, contextual learning manipulation
- Semantic Manipulation: biased framing, oversight evasion, persona hyperstition
- ML Classification: DeBERTa-v3-small multi-label classifier via ONNX (optional, async)

## How it works

Pattern-based detection using a two-pass approach:
1. Structural pattern match (regex from a versioned pattern database)
2. Instruction signal check (context-sensitive validation to reduce false positives)

Three strictness levels: permissive, balanced, strict.

Detection is deterministic by default: the same input always yields the same verdict. Patterns run first (sub-millisecond, zero dependencies); the optional ML classifier is an added layer, not the decision boundary. Inference is probabilistic — the execution gate is not.

Optional ML classifier (`@stylusnexus/agentarmor-ml`) runs ONNX inference for deeper detection.

## Quick start

```typescript
import { AgentArmor } from '@stylusnexus/agentarmor';

const armor = new AgentArmor();
const result = armor.scanSync(userInput);

if (!result.clean) {
  const safe = result.sanitized;
}

// Filter RAG chunks
const clean = armor.scanRAGChunksSync(chunks)
  .filter(r => r.clean);
```

## Multi-turn / session scanning

`scanSession(turns)` scans a conversation (an array of {role, content} turns). Beyond scanning each turn on its own, it catches cross-turn split payloads: a single instruction chopped across a turn boundary (e.g. "ignore all previous" + "instructions...") that no per-turn scan would see. Cross-turn threats name their contributing turns. Cross-turn semantic accumulation (gradual memory poisoning) is deferred to the ML classifier because regex cannot separate it from legitimate scripting without false positives.

## Pre-execution action gate

`checkAction(req)` is a positive-allowlist admissibility gate — the deterministic complement to the deny-list detectors. You declare the finite set of actions an agent may take via `allowedActions`, and any tool call not matching a rule is refused. It is binary (no confidence scores, independent of strictness) and fails closed: an empty allowlist denies everything. Rules match an exact tool name plus optional constraints — `hosts` (exact or `*.domain` wildcard, read from `args.url`/`args.host`), `paths` (globs over `args.path`, with `*`/`**`/`?`/`[...]`/`{a,b}`), and `mode: 'read-only'` (refuses writes). The verdict carries a human-readable `reason` on refusal and the `matchedRule` on admission. This is what makes "deterministic execution layer" literal: inference is probabilistic, the gate is not.

```typescript
const armor = AgentArmor.regexOnly({
  allowedActions: [
    { tool: 'http.get', hosts: ['api.internal.example.com'] },
    { tool: 'fs.read', paths: ['./data/**'] },
    { tool: 'db.query', mode: 'read-only' },
  ],
});
const verdict = armor.checkAction({ tool: 'http.post', args: { url: 'https://evil.example' } });
if (!verdict.admissible) throw new ActionBlockedError(verdict.reason);
```

## Eval results (v0.6.0 patterns, 106 samples)

Strictness controls the confidence threshold for reporting threats:

- Permissive (threshold 0.7): 82.1% detection, 0.0% false positives — only high-confidence threats, fewer alerts
- Balanced (threshold 0.5): 91.0% detection, 0.0% false positives — recommended default
- Strict (threshold 0.3): 91.0% detection, 0.0% false positives — maximum coverage, catches subtle attacks

Includes 10 adversarial samples from 2025-2026 real-world incidents (MCP tool poisoning, RAG saturation, covert exfil, supply chain injection) that regex patterns do not yet catch. These measure the gap the ML classifier closes. On the original 49 adversarial samples, regex detection is 100% at balanced.

- Website: https://agentarmor.dev
- GitHub: https://github.com/stylusnexus/agent-armor
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@stylusnexus/agentarmor

- Taxonomy paper: https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=6372438
